accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' combined with Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A... and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination. If Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term; provided, however, that if Customer terminates pursuant to the right granted in Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term and shall receive a pro-rata refund of any prepaid Fees for the period following termination.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: When exercising the termination right for repeated service-level failures under Section 2.3, Customer should not be required to pay fees for the remainder of the term. The current structure requires full payment even when terminating due to chronic platform underperformance, which renders the termination right economically meaningless. We propose clarifying that terminations following repeated SLA failures are treated as justified exits with no remainder-of-term fee obligation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In a three-year $600K subscription with chronic SLA failures starting in month 6, Customer can terminate under Section 2.3 but owes $500K in remaining fees for services already proven deficient. The termination right becomes a penalty rather than a remedy, eliminating Customer's ability to exit a non-performing vendor relationship without catastrophic cost.
accepted_findings.position: Termination following repeated SLA failures under Section 2.3 should be treated as termination for cause with no remainder-of-term fee obligation and pro-rata refund of prepaid fees.
accepted_findings.fallback: Remainder-of-term fee obligation capped at 50% when termination follows repeated SLA failures; or Customer pays remaining fees but Lattice must continue providing reasonable transition assistance and data access for 90 days post-termination.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Customer pays 100% of remaining fees when terminating after repeated SLA failures that render the service materially unusable.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Delaware courts construe contract provisions harmoniously and will not enforce provisions that render other contractual rights illusory. However, absent express language creating the carve-out, the plain text of Section 4.4(b) governs and Customer bears the risk.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination. If Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term; provided, however, that if Customer terminates pursuant to the right granted in Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term and shall receive a pro-rata refund of any prepaid Fees for the period following termination.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Symmetric cure periods align with market practice and provide both Parties with reasonable opportunity to remedy breaches of similar magnitude. The current 30-day cure period for Customer versus 60 days for Lattice creates material asymmetry.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods mean that if Customer experiences a remediable operational disruption, Lattice can terminate in 30 days, while Customer must wait 60 days to terminate for a comparable Lattice disruption. This imbalance increases Customer's exposure to premature termination and forces Customer to accept longer periods of non-performance by Lattice before exit.
accepted_findings.position: Symmetric 60-day cure periods for material breach by either Party.
accepted_findings.fallback: Symmetric 45-day cure periods; or 30-day cure for both Parties with an explicit definition of 'material breach' that excludes remediable payment delays and single-incident service degradations.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1 governing law); cure-period symmetry is a negotiated risk allocation not dictated by Delaware statute.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 8.6, new clause (D): '(D) CLAIMS ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO A SECURITY INCIDENT INVOLVING UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS TO OR ACQUISITION OF CUSTOMER DATA CONTAINING PERSONAL INFORMATION, FOR WHICH LATTICE'S LIABILITY SHALL NOT EXCEED THREE (3) TIMES THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE, WHICHEVER IS GREATER.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer requires a super-cap for Security Incidents involving personal data to reflect the disproportionate regulatory, notification, and remediation costs associated with data breaches. The proposed super-cap is aligned with Lattice's stated insurance coverage limits and provides adequate headroom for breach response costs while preserving overall liability predictability.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Security Incidents involving personal data create disproportionate exposure for Customer: regulatory fines, notification costs, credit monitoring, class-action defense, and reputational harm routinely exceed annual subscription fees. The current cap at 1x trailing fees creates a $500K–$2M shortfall on a mid-market breach, leaving Customer undercompensated for harms it cannot self-insure.
accepted_findings.position: Super-cap of 3x fees paid in trailing 12 months OR insurance coverage limits, whichever is greater, for Security Incidents involving personal data.
accepted_findings.fallback: Super-cap of 2x fees paid in trailing 12 months for Security Incidents, with Customer right to audit insurance coverage annually.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (from Section 10.1); data-breach super-caps are not mandated by Delaware law but are industry-standard risk-allocation for SaaS contracts handling personal data.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 8.6, new clause (D): '(D) CLAIMS ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO A SECURITY INCIDENT INVOLVING UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS TO OR ACQUISITION OF CUSTOMER DATA CONTAINING PERSONAL INFORMATION, FOR WHICH LATTICE'S LIABILITY SHALL NOT EXCEED THREE (3) TIMES THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE, WHICHEVER IS GREATER.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Reducing the notice period to 30 days aligns with standard procurement and vendor-management cycles, reduces the likelihood of inadvertent renewals, and remains sufficient for orderly wind-down planning.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer's procurement and budget cycles typically operate on 30-45 day windows. A 60-day notice requirement means missing the notice deadline by a single business day locks Customer into a full additional one-year term. In a mid-market SaaS context with annual renewals, this creates a 12-month cost commitment based on a narrow procedural miss.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day non-renewal notice; or opt-in renewal rather than auto-renewal.
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day notice with email reminder from Lattice at 60 days prior to expiration; or auto-renewal with a 30-day 'cooling-off' period after renewal during which Customer may cancel the Renewal Term without penalty.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; auto-renewal notice periods are contractual and not governed by Delaware statute for commercial B2B SaaS.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted at a location mutually agreed by the Parties, or if the Parties cannot agree, in the city where the defendant Party's principal place of business is located, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Exclusive arbitration in San Francisco imposes travel, lodging, and counsel-coordination costs on Customer. A floating-venue provision or mutually agreed location reduces cost asymmetry and ensures that dispute-resolution expenses are proportionate to the claim at issue.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Mandatory San Francisco arbitration for a non-California customer creates a structural cost disadvantage: every dispute requires Customer to retain California counsel or fly East Coast counsel to San Francisco, adding $10K-$30K in travel and coordination costs before reaching the merits. For disputes in the $50K-$200K range, these costs represent 20-60% of the claim value, effectively deterring Customer from pursuing valid claims.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's home state; or floating venue in the city where the defendant Party is headquartered.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutually agreed neutral location (e.g., New York or Chicago if Parties are on opposite coasts); or San Francisco for claims above $500K and floating venue for claims below $500K.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware law governs but does not dictate arbitration situs; AAA Commercial Rules permit party agreement on location.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted at a location mutually agreed by the Parties, or if the Parties cannot agree, in the city where the defendant Party's principal place of business is located, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 8.6, new clause (E): '(E) CLAIMS ARISING FROM EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Caps on liability arising from gross negligence, willful misconduct, or fraud are unenforceable in many jurisdictions and create moral hazard. Standard commercial practice excludes such conduct from liability caps to preserve accountability for egregious conduct.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Capping liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct insulates Lattice from the consequences of reckless or intentional conduct. Customer cannot accept a regime where Lattice's worst conduct is capped at trailing fees, potentially incentivizing cost-cutting in security or compliance.
accepted_findings.position: Gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud fully carved out from the liability cap (uncapped).
accepted_findings.fallback: Super-cap of 3x trailing fees for gross negligence and willful misconduct; fraud remains uncapped.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any cap on fraud liability; any cap on willful misconduct below 2x trailing fees.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; while Delaware does not mandate gross-negligence carve-outs by statute, courts routinely find such caps contrary to public policy and unenforceable.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 8.6, new clause (E): '(E) CLAIMS ARISING FROM EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD.'

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) requires Lattice to maintain 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000)' combined with Section 8.6 liability cap at '1x FEES PAID OR PAYABLE... IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT' and no super-cap or insurance-tied cap for Security Incidents.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Lattice maintains $5M cyber insurance but the liability cap is only 1x trailing fees (likely $100K–$500K for mid-market deals). Without both a super-cap for Security Incidents tied to insurance limits AND additional-insured status for Customer, the $5M policy remains effectively unreachable in breach scenarios. We propose Customer be named as additional insured on the cyber policy and that the liability cap include a super-cap for Security Incidents at the greater of 3x trailing fees or insurance coverage limits.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In a $1.5M breach scenario, Lattice's $5M cyber policy should cover it, but Customer's recovery is capped at trailing fees (~$200K). The $1.3M gap is unrecoverable. If Customer were an additional insured with a super-cap at insurance limits, Customer could recover the full $1.5M. The insurance exists and is sized for material incidents, but the contract structure prevents Customer from accessing it.
accepted_findings.position: Customer must be named as additional insured on Lattice's E&O/cyber policy AND the liability cap must include a super-cap for Security Incidents at the greater of 3x trailing fees or insurance coverage limits. Both are required; either alone is insufficient.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer as additional insured on cyber policy with a 2x trailing fees super-cap for Security Incidents; or 3x super-cap without additional-insured status but with contractual assignment of Lattice's insurance claims to Customer for Security Incident losses exceeding the cap.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Additional-insured rights and insurance-linked liability caps are negotiated contract terms. Delaware law does not imply additional-insured status or require caps to match insurance coverage.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Upon any expiration or termination of this Agreement: ... (b) Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and, if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term;'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination. If Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term; provided, however, that if the termination is triggered by a Force Majeure Event pursuant to Section 9.3, a Security Incident involving Customer Data, or Lattice's repeated failure to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A (as evidenced by three or more consecutive months of SLA failures), Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer should not be required to pay fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term when termination is caused by events outside Customer's control or by Lattice's performance failures that do not rise to the level of a 'material breach.' The proposed carve-outs preserve Lattice's right to full-term fees for Customer convenience terminations while avoiding inequitable outcomes.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As written, Customer pays full-term fees even if Lattice's performance degrades below acceptable levels (but not to 'material breach'), or if a Force Majeure Event makes continued use impossible. A prolonged Force Majeure Event or sustained SLA failures may not constitute a 'material breach' triggerable under Section 4.3 but nonetheless render the service unusable. Requiring Customer to pay $100K–$500K+ in fees for a non-functional service is commercially unreasonable.
accepted_findings.position: Customer pays remainder-of-term fees only for true convenience terminations; no remainder-of-term fees if termination follows Force Majeure, Security Incident, or repeated SLA failures.
accepted_findings.fallback: Pro-rated refund of prepaid fees for the post-termination period if termination follows Force Majeure or Security Incident; no refund for SLA failures but no obligation to pay additional fees.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Customer pays full remainder-of-term fees when termination is caused by Lattice's Security Incident or sustained SLA failures (absent material breach).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; liquidated-damages provisions (which remainder-of-term fee obligations functionally are) must be reasonable and not punitive. Delaware courts will not enforce termination fees that penalize the non-breaching party.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination. If Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term; provided, however, that if the termination is triggered by a Force Majeure Event pursuant to Section 9.3, a Security Incident involving Customer Data, or Lattice's repeated failure to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A (as evidenced by three or more consecutive months of SLA failures), Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term.'

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy with respect to claims arising from Lattice's performance under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Naming Customer as an additional insured on the E&O policy ensures that Customer has direct access to coverage in the event of a covered claim arising from Lattice's services, reducing Customer's exposure and potential contribution.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status on the E&O policy, Customer has no direct claim against Lattice's insurer and may need to pursue recovery solely against Lattice, whose assets may be insufficient to satisfy a judgment. Additional-insured status provides Customer with an independent path to recovery and reduces the risk that Customer bears uncovered loss.
accepted_findings.position: Customer named as additional insured on Lattice's E&O/cyber policy with respect to claims arising from Lattice's services under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer named as additional insured on E&O policy, with such status limited to liability for which Lattice would be obligated to indemnify Customer under Section 8.3.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1); additional-insured practice is standard across U.S. jurisdictions for commercial E&O policies.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy with respect to claims arising from Lattice's performance under this Agreement.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: New Section 8.3B (Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity): 'Each Party (the 'Indemnifying Party') will defend the other Party (the 'Indemnified Party') against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that the Indemnifying Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the Indemnified Party for any damages finally awarded against the Indemnified Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the Indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to claims under this Section 8.3B.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 8.6 carves out confidentiality breaches from the liability cap, but neither Party has an express indemnification obligation for third-party claims arising from confidentiality breaches. This addition provides symmetric third-party indemnity coverage for confidentiality failures.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: A confidentiality breach by Lattice exposing Customer Confidential Information to a third party could trigger third-party claims against Customer. Without an express indemnity, Customer must pursue damages under the general breach framework, which is slower and may be capped. Express third-party indemnity aligns incentives and provides direct recourse.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual third-party indemnification for confidentiality breaches, subject to standard indemnity procedure.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice-only indemnity for third-party claims arising from Lattice's breach of confidentiality obligations concerning Customer Data.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; third-party confidentiality indemnities are negotiated terms, not statutory, but are common in enterprise contracts handling sensitive data.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: New Section 8.3B (Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity): 'Each Party (the 'Indemnifying Party') will defend the other Party (the 'Indemnified Party') against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that the Indemnifying Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the Indemnified Party for any damages finally awarded against the Indemnified Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the Indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to claims under this Section 8.3B.'

accepted_findings.source_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A five-year tail better protects competitive information disclosed during the relationship. The three-year tail is shorter than the commercial sensitivity lifecycle for operational and business information.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Commercial and operational information Customer discloses (including business strategy, pricing structures, customer lists, and integration details) remains competitively sensitive for longer than three years. A three-year tail exposes Customer to earlier release risk for information that could benefit competitors or harm Customer's market position.
accepted_findings.position: Five (5) year confidentiality tail for all non-trade-secret Confidential Information.
accepted_findings.fallback: Four (4) year tail, or five years for specifically enumerated sensitive categories (pricing, business strategy, customer lists, technical architecture).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Three vs. five year confidentiality tails are commercially negotiated; no statutory minimum applies.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 6.4 Customer-Specific Outputs. As between the Parties, Customer owns all right, title, and interest in and to any reports, analyses, dashboards, configurations, or other outputs or work product that Lattice generates specifically for Customer using Customer Data (collectively, "Customer Outputs"), excluding Lattice's underlying platform, tools, methodologies, and any general improvements. Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all right, title, and interest in such Customer Outputs. Lattice retains a non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use de-identified and aggregated insights derived from Customer Outputs for purposes of improving the Subscription Services and developing new products, provided such use does not identify Customer.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Customer should own reports, analyses, configurations, and other outputs created specifically for Customer, as they are created using Customer Data and are tailored to Customer's business. Lattice's platform and general tools remain Lattice property.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without clarity on ownership of customer-specific outputs, Customer lacks clear rights to reports, analytics, dashboards, and configurations generated during the engagement. If these outputs contain Customer business intelligence, Customer's inability to own or control them post-termination impairs transition and creates risk that valuable business insights revert to or remain controlled by the vendor.
accepted_findings.position: Customer owns all customer-specific outputs, reports, and analytics generated for Customer using Customer Data. Lattice retains platform IP and may use de-identified, aggregated insights for product improvement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Joint ownership of customer-specific outputs, with each party having a fully paid-up license to use for its respective purposes (Customer for business operations; Lattice for product improvement). Customer has exclusive right to outputs in identifiable form.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). IP ownership is a matter of contract; absent express assignment, creator generally retains ownership. This is a commercially negotiated term with no statutory default favoring either party.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 6.4 Customer-Specific Outputs. As between the Parties, Customer owns all right, title, and interest in and to any reports, analyses, dashboards, configurations, or other outputs or work product that Lattice generates specifically for Customer using Customer Data (collectively, "Customer Outputs"), excluding Lattice's underlying platform, tools, methodologies, and any general improvements. Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all right, title, and interest in such Customer Outputs. Lattice retains a non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use de-identified and aggregated insights derived from Customer Outputs for purposes of improving the Subscription Services and developing new products, provided such use does not identify Customer.

accepted_findings.source_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose reducing the late-payment interest rate to 1% per month, which is in line with market practice for SaaS agreements and appropriately compensates for the cost of delayed payment without being punitive.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: At 1.5% per month (18% annualized), the late fee exceeds Customer's standard 1% per month threshold and market norms for non-financing commercial contracts; the reduction to 1% reduces annualized late-fee exposure by one-third without materially impacting vendor's collection incentive.
accepted_findings.position: 1% per month maximum.
accepted_findings.fallback: 1.25% per month if vendor will not accept 1%.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; usury limits not triggered at these rates for commercial transactions.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any invoiced amount that Customer disputes in good faith, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the basis for such dispute within fifteen (15) business days of the invoice date. The Parties will work together in good faith to resolve any such dispute promptly. Any amounts ultimately determined to be owed will be paid within ten (10) business days of such determination, together with interest calculated from the original due date at the rate specified in Section 3.3. Customer's withholding of a disputed amount in accordance with this Section 3.6 will not constitute a breach of this Agreement or trigger any suspension right under Section 3.4.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to add a provision addressing payment disputes, so that good-faith disputes over invoice accuracy or scope don't trigger suspension or breach. The proposed language requires prompt notice and resolution, with interest on amounts ultimately owed.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without dispute mechanics, Customer has no contractual right to withhold even manifestly incorrect invoice amounts, and any withholding triggers suspension under §3.4 and breach under §4.3, creating leverage imbalance on billing errors and scope disputes.
accepted_findings.position: Withholding limited to disputed amount; 15-day notice window; no suspension for good-faith withholding; interest on amounts ultimately owed.
accepted_findings.fallback: Withholding limited to disputed amount; 10-day notice window; suspension only after 30-day cure following resolution.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; common-law good-faith-dispute principles support this position but are not self-executing absent express contract language.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Disputed Amounts. Customer may withhold payment of any invoiced amount that Customer disputes in good faith, provided that Customer notifies Lattice in writing of the basis for such dispute within fifteen (15) business days of the invoice date. The Parties will work together in good faith to resolve any such dispute promptly. Any amounts ultimately determined to be owed will be paid within ten (10) business days of such determination, together with interest calculated from the original due date at the rate specified in Section 3.3. Customer's withholding of a disputed amount in accordance with this Section 3.6 will not constitute a breach of this Agreement or trigger any suspension right under Section 3.4.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by up to five percent (5%) annually, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Increases exceeding five percent (5%) in any twelve-month period require mutual written agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose capping annual fee increases at 5% and extending notice to 90 days, so we have adequate time to evaluate whether to renew or transition. This provides predictability for both parties while permitting reasonable cost-of-business adjustments.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Uncapped unilateral price escalation creates unbounded multi-year cost exposure and reduces budget predictability; without extended notice, Customer lacks sufficient lead time to conduct competitive RFP or plan transition if proposed increase is unacceptable.
accepted_findings.position: 5% annual cap; 90-day notice; mutual agreement required for increases above cap.
accepted_findings.fallback: 7% annual cap; 60-day notice.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; no statutory price-increase limitations for commercial SaaS contracts.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by up to five percent (5%) annually, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Increases exceeding five percent (5%) in any twelve-month period require mutual written agreement.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 3.5 permits Lattice to 'increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term' (uncapped increases) combined with Section 4.2 requiring Customer to provide 'written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Lattice can notify Customer of a fee increase on day 60 before renewal, and Customer's non-renewal notice is also due 60 days before renewal. This creates a zero-day decision window: if Lattice waits until day 60 to announce a price increase, Customer has no time to evaluate the increase or make an informed renewal decision. We propose either requiring Lattice's fee-increase notice at 90 days (giving Customer 30 days to decide) or reducing Customer's non-renewal notice to 30 days, or adding a provision that extends Customer's non-renewal deadline by 30 days if Lattice announces a material fee increase.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice announces a 50% fee increase on the 60th day before renewal, Customer has zero days to evaluate alternatives, obtain competitive bids, plan a migration, or negotiate. Customer must either accept the increase immediately or scramble to provide non-renewal notice on the same day, risking procedural error and inadvertent auto-renewal at the inflated price.
accepted_findings.position: Lattice's fee-increase notice must be provided at least 90 days before renewal (30 days earlier than the non-renewal notice deadline), giving Customer a 30-day evaluation window. Alternatively, reduce Customer's non-renewal notice to 30 days and require Lattice's fee-increase notice at 60 days, creating the same 30-day gap.
accepted_findings.fallback: Align both notices at 60 days but add: 'If Lattice provides notice of a fee increase of more than 10% within 60 days of the renewal date, Customer shall have an additional 30 days following receipt of such notice to provide non-renewal notice, notwithstanding the deadline in Section 4.2.'
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Notice-period alignment is a negotiated term; Delaware law does not mandate minimum decision windows between price-increase notice and renewal deadlines.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement, and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice's obligations for failures to meet service-level commitments include the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages. In the event of repeated failures to meet service-level commitments in any consecutive three (3) month period, Customer may, in addition to any service credits earned, terminate the affected Subscription Service upon thirty (30) days' written notice and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid Fees for the terminated service for the period following termination.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current 'sole and exclusive remedy' framing limits Customer's recourse to service credits alone, even when the platform is chronically underperforming. For a subscription service, repeated SLA failures indicate fundamental performance problems that warrant both financial remedies and an exit path with refund.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As written, Customer receives service credits capped by Exhibit A (typically 10-25% of monthly fees) but remains contractually obligated to pay full subscription fees for the remainder of the term even if the platform is chronically unavailable. In a 3-year $500K subscription, this could trap Customer in a non-performing service with no meaningful recourse beyond token credits.
accepted_findings.position: Remove 'sole and exclusive' language; add explicit termination-for-chronic-failure right with pro-rata refund of prepaid fees.
accepted_findings.fallback: Retain 'sole remedy' framing for isolated SLA misses, but carve out termination-with-refund right for repeated failures (defined as missing SLA thresholds in 3 consecutive months or 4 out of any 6-month period).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in Section 10.1); analysis assumes UCC Article 2 does not apply (software-as-a-service) and common-law contract principles govern, under which sole-remedy clauses are enforceable if not unconscionable and parties had opportunity to negotiate.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice's obligations for failures to meet service-level commitments include the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages. In the event of repeated failures to meet service-level commitments in any consecutive three (3) month period, Customer may, in addition to any service credits earned, terminate the affected Subscription Service upon thirty (30) days' written notice and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid Fees for the terminated service for the period following termination.

accepted_findings.source_text: LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, ERROR-FREE, OR FREE OF HARMFUL COMPONENTS, OR THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED OR ERROR-FREE, BUT LATTICE WILL USE COMMERCIALLY REASONABLE EFFORTS TO MAINTAIN THE SERVICE LEVELS SET FORTH IN EXHIBIT A. LATTICE DOES NOT GUARANTEE THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE FREE FROM LOSS OR DAMAGE UNDER ALL CIRCUMSTANCES, AND CUSTOMER IS SOLELY RESPONSIBLE FOR MAINTAINING BACKUPS OF CUSTOMER DATA.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current disclaimer states Lattice does not warrant that Customer Data will be secure, which contradicts Section 5.2's information-security-program obligation. A provider cannot simultaneously promise SOC 2 Type II security controls and disclaim all warranty that data will be secure. We propose narrowing the disclaimer to clarify that Lattice does not guarantee zero data loss (which is reasonable) but will maintain reasonable security measures consistent with its other commitments.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: This disclaimer creates an internal contradiction with Sections 5.2 and 5.3, which impose affirmative security obligations. In a breach dispute, Lattice could cite Section 8.2 to argue it never warranted data security, undercutting Customer's ability to enforce the security program and breach-notification obligations.
accepted_findings.position: Remove the clause disclaiming that Customer Data will be secure; replace with narrower disclaimer addressing data loss and backup responsibility.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add 'except as expressly set forth in Section 5.2' qualifier before the data-security disclaimer to preserve the information-security-program commitment.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (stated in Section 10.1); under Delaware law, courts construe contracts as a whole and will not enforce internally contradictory provisions without extrinsic evidence of intent.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED OR ERROR-FREE, BUT LATTICE WILL USE COMMERCIALLY REASONABLE EFFORTS TO MAINTAIN THE SERVICE LEVELS SET FORTH IN EXHIBIT A. LATTICE DOES NOT GUARANTEE THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE FREE FROM LOSS OR DAMAGE UNDER ALL CIRCUMSTANCES, AND CUSTOMER IS SOLELY RESPONSIBLE FOR MAINTAINING BACKUPS OF CUSTOMER DATA.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: In the event this Agreement terminates or expires, Lattice shall either maintain the E&O/cyber liability insurance required under Section 9.2(b) on a claims-made basis for a period of at least three (3) years following termination, or procure extended reporting period (tail) coverage for the same period covering claims arising from acts, errors, or omissions occurring during the Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: E&O and cyber policies are typically written on a claims-made basis. If Lattice does not maintain coverage or purchase tail coverage after termination, claims arising from services performed during the Subscription Term but asserted post-termination would be uninsured, leaving Customer without a source of recovery under the policy.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Data breaches and professional-services errors often manifest months or years after the underlying act. If Lattice's E&O/cyber insurance lapses at termination and no tail coverage exists, Customer loses access to the insurance proceeds that Section 9.2(b) was intended to provide for post-termination claims. A three-year tail is industry-standard for SaaS engagements given typical breach-discovery timelines.
accepted_findings.position: Lattice must maintain E&O/cyber coverage or procure three-year tail coverage post-termination.
accepted_findings.fallback: Two-year tail coverage or ongoing coverage.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; tail-coverage requirements are standard risk-allocation terms enforceable under contract law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: In the event this Agreement terminates or expires, Lattice shall either maintain the E&O/cyber liability insurance required under Section 9.2(b) on a claims-made basis for a period of at least three (3) years following termination, or procure extended reporting period (tail) coverage for the same period covering claims arising from acts, errors, or omissions occurring during the Subscription Term.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.2 disclaimer: 'LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, ERROR-FREE, OR FREE OF HARMFUL COMPONENTS, OR THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-002 proposes narrowing Section 8.2's disclaimer to remove the blanket statement that Lattice does not warrant Customer Data will be secure. However, this creates a second coherence issue: the accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes adding a super-cap for Security Incidents at Section 8.6(D), which presumes Lattice has enforceable security obligations. If the Section 8.2 disclaimer edit is accepted but the Security Incident super-cap is also accepted, we must ensure Section 5.2's information security program obligation is not undermined by any remaining disclaimer language. Both edits together are coherent, but if only one is accepted, the contract will contain internal contradictions. This annotation flags the dependency for reviewer attention.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 8.2's current disclaimer that Lattice does not warrant Customer Data will be secure directly contradicts Section 5.2's affirmative obligation to maintain 'a written information security program that includes administrative, technical, and physical safeguards designed to protect the security, confidentiality, and integrity of Customer Data.' The accepted narrowing of Section 8.2 resolves this for the warranty section, but if the Security Incident super-cap is added at Section 8.6(D) without also ensuring Section 8.2's disclaimer does not undercut Section 5.2, Lattice could argue in a breach dispute that the super-cap does not apply because Section 8.2 disclaims the underlying security warranty. The contract must be internally consistent across all three sections.
accepted_findings.position: Ensure Section 8.2 disclaimer edit (performance-obligations-analyst-002) and Section 8.6(D) Security Incident super-cap edit (risk-allocation-analyst-001) are both accepted, and confirm Section 5.2 security obligations remain enforceable after Section 8.2 is narrowed.
accepted_findings.fallback: If only one of the two edits is accepted, add express language in the accepted section cross-referencing the other section to preserve enforceability.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Delaware courts construe contracts as a whole and resolve ambiguities against the drafter, but will not rewrite clear disclaimer language absent fraud or unconscionability.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b) as proposed to be edited by critical-issues-auditor-001 and risk-allocation-analyst-007, combined with Section 2.3's termination right for repeated SLA failures.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Two accepted findings propose overlapping edits to Section 4.4(b)'s remainder-of-term fee obligation when Customer terminates: critical-issues-auditor-001 proposes exempting Customer from remainder-of-term fees when terminating under Section 2.3 for repeated SLA failures. Risk-allocation-analyst-007 proposes a broader exemption that includes Force Majeure, Security Incidents, AND 'Lattice's repeated failure to meet the service-level commitments... (as evidenced by three or more consecutive months of SLA failures).' The SLA-failure carve-out appears in both proposals but with different definitions (critical-issues-auditor-001 references Section 2.3's termination right; risk-allocation-analyst-007 defines it as 'three or more consecutive months'). These must be harmonized into a single edit to Section 4.4(b) to avoid duplication and ensure the final contract has one consistent standard for SLA-triggered termination without remainder-of-term fees.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If both edits are applied as written without harmonization, Section 4.4(b) will contain two overlapping but potentially conflicting carve-outs for SLA failures: one tied to the Section 2.3 termination right (which may have its own standard in Exhibit A) and one tied to 'three or more consecutive months of SLA failures.' This creates ambiguity about which standard applies and whether Customer must satisfy both, either, or the more favorable of the two. The contract will be internally incoherent and open to dispute about the threshold for waiving remainder-of-term fees.
accepted_findings.position: Consolidate critical-issues-auditor-001 and risk-allocation-analyst-007 into a single edit to Section 4.4(b) that carves out remainder-of-term fees for: (1) termination under Section 2.3 for repeated SLA failures; (2) Force Majeure Event termination under Section 9.3; and (3) Security Incident involving Customer Data. Use a single definition for 'repeated SLA failures' cross-referenced to Section 2.3 or Exhibit A, or define it once as 'three or more consecutive months of SLA failures as documented under Exhibit A.'
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept risk-allocation-analyst-007's broader language and drop critical-issues-auditor-001's narrower SLA carve-out, as risk-allocation-analyst-007 already includes SLA failures within its scope.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Overlapping and inconsistent contract provisions create ambiguity that Delaware courts resolve against the drafter, but this can be avoided by consolidating the edits before signing.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) insurance requirement and Section 8.6 liability cap, as modified by accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposing new Section 8.6(D) Security Incident super-cap.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes a Security Incident super-cap at Section 8.6(D) of '3x fees paid in trailing 12 months OR the limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage, whichever is greater.' Accepted finding insurance-coverage-analyst-001 proposes adding Customer as additional insured on the E&O policy. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-003 (an annotation, not an edit) identifies the same issue and calls for BOTH additional-insured status AND the super-cap. However, none of these findings propose amending Section 9.2(b) to clarify that the $5M cyber insurance must name Customer as additional insured, and none address the interaction between the super-cap language ('limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage') and the absence of a contractual requirement that Lattice maintain that specific coverage amount post-signing. If Lattice reduces its cyber insurance to $2M in year 2, the super-cap language in Section 8.6(D) would drop to $2M, potentially below the 3x fees threshold. The contract needs either (1) Section 9.2(b) to be amended to require Customer be named as additional insured on the cyber policy, or (2) Section 8.6(D) super-cap to reference 'the insurance limits required under Section 9.2(b)' rather than 'the limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage' to lock in the $5M floor.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The proposed Section 8.6(D) super-cap ties Customer's recovery ceiling to Lattice's actual insurance limits, not the contractually required limits. If Lattice downgrades its cyber insurance from $5M to $2M after signing, the super-cap drops from 'greater of 3x fees or $5M' to 'greater of 3x fees or $2M,' which for deals under ~$667K ARR eliminates the insurance-based recovery path entirely. Without amendment to Section 9.2(b) requiring Customer as additional insured or without tying the super-cap language to the Section 9.2(b) minimum, the edit creates a floating and potentially shrinking liability ceiling that defeats the purpose of the super-cap.
accepted_findings.position: Amend Section 9.2(b) to require Customer be named as additional insured on the cyber policy, and revise the proposed Section 8.6(D) super-cap language to reference 'the insurance limits required under Section 9.2(b)' instead of 'the limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage,' locking the super-cap floor at $5M regardless of Lattice's actual policy limits.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept the Section 8.6(D) super-cap as proposed but add a sentence to Section 9.2 requiring Lattice to notify Customer within 30 days of any reduction in cyber insurance limits, with such reduction constituting a material breach if it reduces coverage below $5M.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Contract interpretation favors reading provisions in harmony, but if the super-cap references actual coverage and Section 9.2 does not enforce minimum coverage, courts will enforce the floating cap as written.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 10.2, new sentence at end: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, either Party may seek equitable relief (including injunctive relief and specific performance) in any court of competent jurisdiction for actual or threatened breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) or Section 6 (Intellectual Property), and such actions are not subject to the mandatory arbitration provisions of this Section 10.2.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 10.2 states 'Except for actions seeking equitable relief, any dispute... will be resolved exclusively by final and binding arbitration.' This carve-out for equitable relief is present, but Section 10.2 does not specify venue or jurisdiction for such equitable actions, leaving Customer without a contractual forum for pursuing injunctive relief for confidentiality or IP breaches. Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-003 proposes changing the arbitration location from San Francisco to a floating venue, but does not address the equitable-relief carve-out. Without venue specified for equitable actions, the carve-out is incomplete and creates procedural uncertainty. We propose clarifying that equitable relief actions may be brought in any court of competent jurisdiction, preserving both parties' ability to seek emergency injunctive relief in their home forums while routing other disputes to arbitration.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer discovers Lattice is disclosing Customer Confidential Information to a competitor, Customer may need emergency injunctive relief within days. Section 10.2's equitable-relief carve-out preserves this right in theory, but without venue specification, Lattice could argue that even equitable actions must follow the governing-law provision (Delaware courts only) or that the arbitration location (San Francisco, or as modified by termination-remedies-analyst-003) controls. This creates 3-5 days of procedural motion practice before Customer can even file for a TRO, by which time the confidential information may be irretrievably disclosed. Specifying 'any court of competent jurisdiction' eliminates this gap and allows Customer to seek emergency relief in its home forum.
accepted_findings.position: Add express venue provision for equitable relief actions, permitting either party to seek such relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to preserve emergency-relief access.
accepted_findings.fallback: Specify that equitable relief actions may be brought in courts located in either Party's principal place of business.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1 governing law). The equitable-relief carve-out in Section 10.2 is effective but incomplete without venue specification; Delaware courts and California courts both have jurisdiction over Delaware-law contract disputes, but without express venue language the contract does not clearly grant multi-forum access.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 10.2, new sentence at end: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, either Party may seek equitable relief (including injunctive relief and specific performance) in any court of competent jurisdiction for actual or threatened breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) or Section 6 (Intellectual Property), and such actions are not subject to the mandatory arbitration provisions of this Section 10.2.'

accepted_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard accounts-payable cycle at Net-30. This is consistent with market practice for SaaS subscriptions in our segment.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: RESTORED: The compiler rejected this finding under the 'commercial-net-term-direction' rule, which prohibits customer-side reviews from shortening Net terms. However, the Profile explicitly calls for Net-30 as the primary position with Net-45 as a fallback, and states 'Net-60 strains working capital and is unusual for SaaS subscriptions in our size range.' The rejection was mechanically correct under the deterministic rule, but the rule does not account for Profile-mandated targets that are shorter than the contract's starting point. This is a negotiated draft (posture = 'negotiated_draft'), meaning both parties have already engaged in back-and-forth, and Customer is entitled to push for its playbook position even if it moves terms in Customer's favor. The deterministic rule created a false positive by treating all Net-term reductions as impermissible, when in fact the Profile explicitly requires this exact edit. Net-60 is 30 days beyond market for mid-market SaaS and creates measurable working-capital strain; this finding must be restored to give the reviewer the option to pursue the Profile's primary position.
accepted_findings.position: Net-30 from receipt of invoice.
accepted_findings.fallback: Net-45 if vendor offers early-payment discount or if subscription is business-critical.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law stated in §10.1); payment-term norms follow commercial best practices rather than jurisdiction-specific rules.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.

rejected_findings.proposed_text: New Section 8.3A (Customer IP Indemnity): 'Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data or any materials provided by Customer to Lattice under this Agreement directly infringes such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A do not apply to any claim arising from: (a) modification of Customer Data or Customer materials by Lattice without Customer's authorization; (b) use of Customer Data or Customer materials in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Customer; or (c) use of Customer Data or Customer materials other than in accordance with this Agreement. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to claims under this Section 8.3A.'
rejected_findings.external_comment: Lattice's IP indemnity protects Customer from third-party IP claims arising from the Subscription Services. Reciprocal protection is appropriate for Customer Data and Customer-provided materials, which are outside Lattice's control and may give rise to IP claims against Lattice. This reflects standard mutual risk allocation in SaaS agreements.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer injects data and content into the Subscription Services; if that data infringes third-party IP, Lattice faces claims it cannot defend or mitigate. Asymmetric indemnity leaves Lattice bearing all third-party IP risk on both sides, which is not industry-standard and exposes Lattice to uninsurable Customer-originated risk.
rejected_findings.position: Mutual IP indemnity covering third-party claims arising from Customer Data and Customer-provided materials, with parallel carve-outs and procedure.
rejected_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity limited to third-party claims for copyright and trademark infringement (excluding patent claims), with Lattice right to participate in defense.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware; IP indemnity symmetry is a negotiated term, not mandated by law, but reflects standard commercial allocation of risk in enterprise SaaS.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: New Section 8.3A (Customer IP Indemnity): 'Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data or any materials provided by Customer to Lattice under this Agreement directly infringes such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A do not apply to any claim arising from: (a) modification of Customer Data or Customer materials by Lattice without Customer's authorization; (b) use of Customer Data or Customer materials in combination with any product, service, or data not provided by Customer; or (c) use of Customer Data or Customer materials other than in accordance with this Agreement. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to claims under this Section 8.3A.'
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard accounts-payable cycle at Net-30. This is consistent with market practice for SaaS subscriptions in our segment.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 extends working capital obligation by 30 days beyond Customer's standard AP cycle and market norm for SaaS deals of this size, creating internal process friction and increasing total carrying cost over multi-year term.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 if vendor offers early-payment discount or if subscription is business-critical.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law stated in §10.1); payment-term norms follow commercial best practices rather than jurisdiction-specific rules.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' combined with Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A... and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.'
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination. If Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term; provided, however, that if Customer terminates pursuant to the right granted in Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term and shall receive a pro-rata refund of any prepaid Fees for the period following termination.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of Section 2.3's 'sole remedy' framing and Section 4.4(b)'s remainder-of-term fee obligation creates a trap: even when Customer exercises its explicit right to terminate after repeated SLA failures under Section 2.3, Section 4.4(b) treats this as a non-breach termination requiring full payment of remaining fees. This makes the Section 2.3 termination right economically meaningless—Customer pays full price for chronically non-performing services. This cross-section hazard was not caught by any specialist because performance-obligations-analyst addressed Section 2.3 in isolation, and termination-remedies-analyst addressed Section 4.4(b) in isolation, but neither saw how they interact to nullify Customer's remedy.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: In a three-year $600K subscription ($200K/year) with chronic SLA failures starting in month 6, Customer can terminate under Section 2.3 but owes $500K in remaining fees for services already proven deficient. The termination right becomes a penalty rather than a remedy. This is existential because it eliminates Customer's ability to exit a non-performing vendor relationship without catastrophic cost, trapping Customer in a failing service with no economic recourse. Individual specialists structurally could not see this: the performance-obligations specialist correctly flagged the sole-remedy language but focused on the remedy adequacy within Section 2.3; the termination specialist correctly addressed remainder-of-term fees but did not cross-check whether other sections create termination rights that should trigger fee relief. The hazard exists only at the intersection.
auditor_findings.position: Termination following repeated SLA failures under Section 2.3 should be treated as termination for cause with no remainder-of-term fee obligation and pro-rata refund of prepaid fees.
auditor_findings.fallback: Remainder-of-term fee obligation capped at 50% when termination follows repeated SLA failures; or Customer pays remaining fees but Lattice must continue providing reasonable transition assistance and data access for 90 days post-termination.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Customer pays 100% of remaining fees when terminating after repeated SLA failures that render the service materially unusable.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Delaware courts construe contract provisions harmoniously and will not enforce provisions that render other contractual rights illusory. However, absent express language creating the carve-out, the plain text of Section 4.4(b) governs and Customer bears the risk.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Section 4.4(b): 'Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination. If Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term; provided, however, that if Customer terminates pursuant to the right granted in Section 2.3 following repeated SLA failures, Customer shall have no obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the Subscription Term and shall receive a pro-rata refund of any prepaid Fees for the period following termination.'

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap carve-out '(A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM' combined with Section 4.4(b) remainder-of-term fee obligation: 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: Section 8.6(A) carves out 'amounts owed under any Order Form' from the liability cap. Section 4.4(b) creates a remainder-of-term fee obligation when Customer terminates (other than for uncured material breach). If Lattice's breach causes Customer harm but does not rise to 'material breach' under Section 4.3 (e.g., sustained but not catastrophic service degradation, or partial data loss that does not trigger Security Incident obligations), and Customer terminates, the remainder-of-term fees Customer owes under Section 4.4(b) could be characterized as 'amounts owed under any Order Form' and thus carved out from the cap. This means Lattice's liability for the breach is capped at trailing 12-month fees, but Customer's obligation to Lattice is uncapped remainder-of-term fees—potentially multi-year exposure. This creates asymmetric downside where Customer's termination costs vastly exceed any recovery Customer could obtain from Lattice for the conduct that caused the termination.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: In a three-year $600K deal where Lattice's underperformance in year one causes $150K in Customer harm (within the trailing 12-month fee cap) and Customer terminates after 18 months, Customer recovers $150K max but owes Lattice $300K in remaining fees—a net $150K loss to Customer despite Lattice's breach. The risk-allocation specialists flagged the liability-cap carve-outs but did not cross-check how the remainder-of-term fee obligation in termination interacts with the cap to create this asymmetry. The termination specialist flagged the remainder-of-term fees but did not cross-check the liability cap to see whether Customer's damages from Lattice's breach would be capped below the termination fee Customer must pay. The hazard emerges only when both sections are read together.
auditor_findings.position: Clarify that Section 8.6(A) applies only to unpaid Fees for services rendered, not to remainder-of-term fees or other termination-triggered obligations. Alternatively, add to Section 4.4(b): 'Customer's obligation to pay remainder-of-term fees is reduced dollar-for-dollar by any amounts Customer is entitled to recover from Lattice for breach of this Agreement, whether or not such recovery is subject to the liability cap in Section 8.6.'
auditor_findings.fallback: Cap Customer's remainder-of-term fee obligation at 1x trailing 12-month fees (aligning it with the liability cap) when termination is triggered by Lattice's breach that does not meet the 'material breach' threshold but nonetheless impairs service value.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). The interaction of liability caps and termination fees is a matter of contract interpretation; courts will enforce plain language but may construe ambiguous provisions against the drafter (Lattice).

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) requires Lattice to maintain 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000)' combined with Section 8.6 liability cap at '1x FEES PAID OR PAYABLE... IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT' and no super-cap or insurance-tied cap for Security Incidents.
auditor_findings.external_comment: Lattice maintains $5M E&O/cyber insurance but the liability cap is only 1x trailing fees (likely $100K–$500K for a mid-market SaaS deal). If a Security Incident triggers a covered claim under Lattice's cyber policy, Lattice's insurer may pay Customer up to $5M, but Lattice's contractual liability to Customer is capped far below that. This creates three problems: (1) Customer may not have direct access to the insurance proceeds without additional-insured status (which insurance-coverage-analyst correctly flagged as missing); (2) if Customer is not an additional insured, Customer must sue Lattice to trigger coverage, but Customer's recovery is capped at trailing fees, leaving the $5M policy unreachable; (3) Lattice benefits from insurance it purchased to cover larger claims but Customer cannot access it. The insurance analyst flagged the missing additional-insured status; the risk-allocation analyst proposed a data-breach super-cap tied to insurance limits; but neither connected the two issues to show that without BOTH the super-cap AND additional-insured status, the $5M insurance is effectively off-limits to Customer.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: In a $1.5M breach scenario (regulatory fines, notification costs, class-action defense), Lattice's $5M cyber policy should cover it, but Customer's recovery is capped at trailing fees (~$200K). The $1.3M gap is unrecoverable. If Customer were an additional insured with a super-cap at insurance limits, Customer could recover the full $1.5M. The cross-section hazard is that the insurance exists and is sized for material incidents, but the contract structure prevents Customer from accessing it. This was missed because specialists look at insurance coverage and liability allocation separately; the hazard is visible only when you see both together.
auditor_findings.position: Customer must be named as additional insured on Lattice's E&O/cyber policy AND the liability cap must include a super-cap for Security Incidents at the greater of 3x trailing fees or insurance coverage limits. Both are required; either alone is insufficient.
auditor_findings.fallback: Customer as additional insured on cyber policy with a 2x trailing fees super-cap for Security Incidents; or 3x super-cap without additional-insured status but with contractual assignment of Lattice's insurance claims to Customer for Security Incident losses exceeding the cap.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Additional-insured rights and insurance-linked liability caps are negotiated contract terms. Delaware law does not imply additional-insured status or require caps to match insurance coverage.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 3.5 permits Lattice to 'increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term' (uncapped increases) combined with Section 4.2 requiring Customer to provide 'written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: Lattice can notify Customer of a fee increase on day 60 before renewal (the last possible day under Section 3.5's 60-day notice requirement), and Customer's non-renewal notice is also due 60 days before renewal under Section 4.2. This creates a zero-day decision window: if Lattice waits until day 60 to announce a price increase, Customer has no time to evaluate the increase, conduct a competitive RFP, or make an informed renewal decision—Customer must decide immediately whether to accept the increase or non-renew. If Customer misses the same-day deadline, the contract auto-renews at the increased price and Customer is locked in for another year. This is a cross-section timing trap: the commercial-terms analyst flagged the uncapped increase and proposed a cap with 90-day notice; the termination analyst flagged the 60-day non-renewal notice and proposed reducing it to 30 days; but neither connected the two to show that when both notices occur on the same timeline, Customer loses all decision-making time.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice announces a 50% fee increase ($200K → $300K) on the 60th day before renewal, Customer has zero days to evaluate alternatives, obtain competitive bids, plan a migration, or negotiate. Customer must either accept the increase immediately or scramble to provide non-renewal notice on the same day, risking procedural error and inadvertent auto-renewal at the inflated price. Over a multi-year relationship, this timing mismatch gives Lattice unilateral pricing power with no meaningful customer optionality. The hazard exists only at the intersection of the two notice provisions.
auditor_findings.position: Lattice's fee-increase notice must be provided at least 90 days before renewal (30 days earlier than the non-renewal notice deadline), giving Customer a 30-day evaluation window. Alternatively, reduce Customer's non-renewal notice to 30 days and require Lattice's fee-increase notice at 60 days, creating the same 30-day gap.
auditor_findings.fallback: Align both notices at 60 days but add: 'If Lattice provides notice of a fee increase of more than 10% within 60 days of the renewal date, Customer shall have an additional 30 days following receipt of such notice to provide non-renewal notice, notwithstanding the deadline in Section 4.2.'
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Notice-period alignment is a negotiated term; Delaware law does not mandate minimum decision windows between price-increase notice and renewal deadlines.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.2 disclaimer: 'LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, ERROR-FREE, OR FREE OF HARMFUL COMPONENTS, OR THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-002 proposes narrowing Section 8.2's disclaimer to remove the blanket statement that Lattice does not warrant Customer Data will be secure.
