However, this creates a second coherence issue: the accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes adding a super-cap for Security Incidents at Section 8.6(D), which presumes Lattice has enforceable security obligations. If the Section 8.2 disclaimer edit is accepted but the Security Incident super-cap is also accepted, we must ensure Section 5.2's information security program obligation is not undermined by any remaining disclaimer language. Both edits together are coherent, but if only one is accepted, the contract will contain internal contradictions. This annotation flags the dependency for reviewer attention.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 8.2's current disclaimer that Lattice does not warrant Customer Data will be secure directly contradicts Section 5.2's affirmative obligation to maintain 'a written information security program that includes administrative, technical, and physical safeguards designed to protect the security, confidentiality, and integrity of Customer Data.' The accepted narrowing of Section 8.2 resolves this for the warranty section, but if the Security Incident super-cap is added at Section 8.6(D) without also ensuring Section 8.2's disclaimer does not undercut Section 5.2, Lattice could argue in a breach dispute that the super-cap does not apply because Section 8.2 disclaims the underlying security warranty. The contract must be internally consistent across all three sections.
coherence_findings.position: Ensure Section 8.2 disclaimer edit (performance-obligations-analyst-002) and Section 8.6(D) Security Incident super-cap edit (risk-allocation-analyst-001) are both accepted, and confirm Section 5.2 security obligations remain enforceable after Section 8.2 is narrowed.
coherence_findings.fallback: If only one of the two edits is accepted, add express language in the accepted section cross-referencing the other section to preserve enforceability.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Delaware courts construe contracts as a whole and resolve ambiguities against the drafter, but will not rewrite clear disclaimer language absent fraud or unconscionability.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b) as proposed to be edited by critical-issues-auditor-001 and risk-allocation-analyst-007, combined with Section 2.3's termination right for repeated SLA failures.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Two accepted findings propose overlapping edits to Section 4.4(b)'s remainder-of-term fee obligation when Customer terminates: critical-issues-auditor-001 proposes exempting Customer from remainder-of-term fees when terminating under Section 2.3 for repeated SLA failures. Risk-allocation-analyst-007 proposes a broader exemption that includes Force Majeure, Security Incidents, AND 'Lattice's repeated failure to meet the service-level commitments... (as evidenced by three or more consecutive months of SLA failures).' The SLA-failure carve-out appears in both proposals but with different definitions (critical-issues-auditor-001 references Section 2.3's termination right; risk-allocation-analyst-007 defines it as 'three or more consecutive months'). These must be harmonized into a single edit to Section 4.4(b) to avoid duplication and ensure the final contract has one consistent standard for SLA-triggered termination without remainder-of-term fees.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If both edits are applied as written without harmonization, Section 4.4(b) will contain two overlapping but potentially conflicting carve-outs for SLA failures: one tied to the Section 2.3 termination right (which may have its own standard in Exhibit A) and one tied to 'three or more consecutive months of SLA failures.' This creates ambiguity about which standard applies and whether Customer must satisfy both, either, or the more favorable of the two. The contract will be internally incoherent and open to dispute about the threshold for waiving remainder-of-term fees.
coherence_findings.position: Consolidate critical-issues-auditor-001 and risk-allocation-analyst-007 into a single edit to Section 4.4(b) that carves out remainder-of-term fees for: (1) termination under Section 2.3 for repeated SLA failures; (2) Force Majeure Event termination under Section 9.3; and (3) Security Incident involving Customer Data. Use a single definition for 'repeated SLA failures' cross-referenced to Section 2.3 or Exhibit A, or define it once as 'three or more consecutive months of SLA failures as documented under Exhibit A.'
coherence_findings.fallback: Accept risk-allocation-analyst-007's broader language and drop critical-issues-auditor-001's narrower SLA carve-out, as risk-allocation-analyst-007 already includes SLA failures within its scope.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Overlapping and inconsistent contract provisions create ambiguity that Delaware courts resolve against the drafter, but this can be avoided by consolidating the edits before signing.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) insurance requirement and Section 8.6 liability cap, as modified by accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposing new Section 8.6(D) Security Incident super-cap.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes a Security Incident super-cap at Section 8.6(D) of '3x fees paid in trailing 12 months OR the limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage, whichever is greater.' Accepted finding insurance-coverage-analyst-001 proposes adding Customer as additional insured on the E&O policy. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-003 (an annotation, not an edit) identifies the same issue and calls for BOTH additional-insured status AND the super-cap. However, none of these findings propose amending Section 9.2(b) to clarify that the $5M cyber insurance must name Customer as additional insured, and none address the interaction between the super-cap language ('limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage') and the absence of a contractual requirement that Lattice maintain that specific coverage amount post-signing. If Lattice reduces its cyber insurance to $2M in year 2, the super-cap language in Section 8.6(D) would drop to $2M, potentially below the 3x fees threshold. The contract needs either (1) Section 9.2(b) to be amended to require Customer be named as additional insured on the cyber policy, or (2) Section 8.6(D) super-cap to reference 'the insurance limits required under Section 9.2(b)' rather than 'the limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage' to lock in the $5M floor.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The proposed Section 8.6(D) super-cap ties Customer's recovery ceiling to Lattice's actual insurance limits, not the contractually required limits. If Lattice downgrades its cyber insurance from $5M to $2M after signing, the super-cap drops from 'greater of 3x fees or $5M' to 'greater of 3x fees or $2M,' which for deals under ~$667K ARR eliminates the insurance-based recovery path entirely. Without amendment to Section 9.2(b) requiring Customer as additional insured or without tying the super-cap language to the Section 9.2(b) minimum, the edit creates a floating and potentially shrinking liability ceiling that defeats the purpose of the super-cap.
coherence_findings.position: Amend Section 9.2(b) to require Customer be named as additional insured on the cyber policy, and revise the proposed Section 8.6(D) super-cap language to reference 'the insurance limits required under Section 9.2(b)' instead of 'the limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage,' locking the super-cap floor at $5M regardless of Lattice's actual policy limits.
coherence_findings.fallback: Accept the Section 8.6(D) super-cap as proposed but add a sentence to Section 9.2 requiring Lattice to notify Customer within 30 days of any reduction in cyber insurance limits, with such reduction constituting a material breach if it reduces coverage below $5M.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1). Contract interpretation favors reading provisions in harmony, but if the super-cap references actual coverage and Section 9.2 does not enforce minimum coverage, courts will enforce the floating cap as written.

coherence_findings.proposed_text: Section 10.2, new sentence at end: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, either Party may seek equitable relief (including injunctive relief and specific performance) in any court of competent jurisdiction for actual or threatened breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) or Section 6 (Intellectual Property), and such actions are not subject to the mandatory arbitration provisions of this Section 10.2.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: Section 10.2 states 'Except for actions seeking equitable relief, any dispute... will be resolved exclusively by final and binding arbitration.' This carve-out for equitable relief is present, but Section 10.2 does not specify venue or jurisdiction for such equitable actions, leaving Customer without a contractual forum for pursuing injunctive relief for confidentiality or IP breaches. Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-003 proposes changing the arbitration location from San Francisco to a floating venue, but does not address the equitable-relief carve-out. Without venue specified for equitable actions, the carve-out is incomplete and creates procedural uncertainty. We propose clarifying that equitable relief actions may be brought in any court of competent jurisdiction, preserving both parties' ability to seek emergency injunctive relief in their home forums while routing other disputes to arbitration.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer discovers Lattice is disclosing Customer Confidential Information to a competitor, Customer may need emergency injunctive relief within days. Section 10.2's equitable-relief carve-out preserves this right in theory, but without venue specification, Lattice could argue that even equitable actions must follow the governing-law provision (Delaware courts only) or that the arbitration location (San Francisco, or as modified by termination-remedies-analyst-003) controls. This creates 3-5 days of procedural motion practice before Customer can even file for a TRO, by which time the confidential information may be irretrievably disclosed. Specifying 'any court of competent jurisdiction' eliminates this gap and allows Customer to seek emergency relief in its home forum.
coherence_findings.position: Add express venue provision for equitable relief actions, permitting either party to seek such relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to preserve emergency-relief access.
coherence_findings.fallback: Specify that equitable relief actions may be brought in courts located in either Party's principal place of business.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1 governing law). The equitable-relief carve-out in Section 10.2 is effective but incomplete without venue specification; Delaware courts and California courts both have jurisdiction over Delaware-law contract disputes, but without express venue language the contract does not clearly grant multi-forum access.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Section 10.2, new sentence at end: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, either Party may seek equitable relief (including injunctive relief and specific performance) in any court of competent jurisdiction for actual or threatened breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) or Section 6 (Intellectual Property), and such actions are not subject to the mandatory arbitration provisions of this Section 10.2.'

restored_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard accounts-payable cycle at Net-30. This is consistent with market practice for SaaS subscriptions in our segment.
restored_findings.materiality_rationale: RESTORED: The compiler rejected this finding under the 'commercial-net-term-direction' rule, which prohibits customer-side reviews from shortening Net terms. However, the Profile explicitly calls for Net-30 as the primary position with Net-45 as a fallback, and states 'Net-60 strains working capital and is unusual for SaaS subscriptions in our size range.' The rejection was mechanically correct under the deterministic rule, but the rule does not account for Profile-mandated targets that are shorter than the contract's starting point. This is a negotiated draft (posture = 'negotiated_draft'), meaning both parties have already engaged in back-and-forth, and Customer is entitled to push for its playbook position even if it moves terms in Customer's favor. The deterministic rule created a false positive by treating all Net-term reductions as impermissible, when in fact the Profile explicitly requires this exact edit. Net-60 is 30 days beyond market for mid-market SaaS and creates measurable working-capital strain; this finding must be restored to give the reviewer the option to pursue the Profile's primary position.
restored_findings.fallback: Net-45 if vendor offers early-payment discount or if subscription is business-critical.
restored_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law stated in §10.1); payment-term norms follow commercial best practices rather than jurisdiction-specific rules.
restored_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.

Section 4.3: 'Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.'
Section 4.3 provides Customer with 30-day cure and Lattice with 60-day cure, creating asymmetry that disfavors Customer.

No provision grants either Party the right to terminate for convenience without cause.
